name: Cecil Payne
abstract: Cecil Payne (December 14, 1922 – November 27, 2007) was an American jazz baritone saxophonist born in Brooklyn, New York. Payne also played the alto saxophone and flute.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Temperley
about_quotes.evidence: I’m very fond of Cecil Payne, and he’s a very good personal friend of mine.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Temperley interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Cecil Payne? [INTERVIEWEE] No, it wasn't Cecil Payne.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: But on there was Clifford Brown, Idrees Sulieman, Gigi Gryce, Benny Golson, Cecil Payne I think... We worked with Bass Ashford... Tadd hired me, Clifford Brown, Johnny Coles, Cecil Payne, Jymie Merritt, Philly Joe Jones, and a trombone player.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Moody
about_quotes.evidence: Cecil Payne [phonetic sp.], Ernie Henry [phonetic sp.]. Yeah. So. And I'm -- I was 21 years old... Donald Cole [phonetic sp.] was a trombone player and Cecil Payne [phonetic sp.] was the baritone player, I think.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Moody — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Wendy Oxenhorn
about_quotes.evidence: I just fell in love with this guy. ... I really got to know you and I really adore you ... Me and Cecil were like this.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendy Oxenhorn — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Moody
about_quotes.evidence: When I joined Dizzy’s band in ‘46... Sol was the baritone player and then later Cecil Payne came in.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Moody interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 13, 1998
